item.title: Spatial–spectral feature guidance network for fine-grained object detection and recognition on complex-valued SAR images
item.dc:creator: Yuxi Suo
Youming Wu
Wenhui Diao
Qingbiao Meng
Xian Sun
Kun Fu
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Single-look complex (SLC) SAR images exhibit the dual nature of imagery and signals, where the former embodies spatial visual features for localization, and the latter contains abundant spectral information to characterize electromagnetic scattering properties. Mainstream object detection and recognition methods rely on intensity images, which represent the integration of backscattered energy across the entire signal bandwidth. On intensity images, backscattered energy at different frequencies cannot be resolved, and the electromagnetic scattering characteristics cannot be effectively characterized. Therefore, the dimensionality of the feature space is constrained, and the performance of object detection and recognition is limited. Upon Fourier transform, the complex-valued signals exhibit the potential to resolve distinct frequency components with high spectral resolution. SLC SAR images hence demonstrate the potential to characterize electromagnetic scattering properties of ground objects and form high-dimensional feature representations to enhance discriminability. However, backscattered energy from different object instances are dispersed and overlapped in the frequency domain, making it challenging to isolate the spectral features of individual objects. This paper proposes a Spatial–Spectral Feature Guidance Network (SSFG-Net) that simultaneously leverages the nature of imagery and signal to form high-dimensional and discriminative features for the improvement of object detection and recognition. The core ideal is to construct a spatial–spectral feature cube, which extends single-dimensional intensity scalars into high-dimensional spectral features via spectral decomposition, forming discriminative features while preserving the spatial position information. Additionally, a Visual Feature Extraction Module (VFE) is designed to complement delicate visual features and model object instances with 2-dimensional Gaussian distributions for the holistic perception of object instances. Finally, a Correlation-Aware Feature Mixer (CAFM) is developed to compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between spatial–spectral and visual features, enabling efficient feature fusion based on the correlation analysis. Experiments on the FAIR-CSAR dataset show that SSFG-Net boosts average AP50 by 4.6% on spotlight mode and 9.1% on strip-map mode, compared to the baseline. The code is available at https://github.com/suoyuxi/SSFG . Published: 2026-03-31T07:19:38+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.857 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuxi Suo; Youming Wu; Wenhui Diao; Qingbiao Meng; Xian Sun; Kun Fu

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.047

Relevance: 0.857 (must_read)

Single-look complex (SLC) SAR images exhibit the dual nature of imagery and signals, where the former embodies spatial visual features for localization, and the latter contains abundant spectral information to characterize electromagnetic scattering properties. Mainstream object detection and recognition methods rely on intensity images, which represent the integration of backscattered energy across the entire signal bandwidth. On intensity images, backscattered energy at different frequencies cannot be resolved, and the electromagnetic scattering characteristics cannot be effectively characterized. Therefore, the dimensionality of the feature space is constrained, and the performance of object detection and recognition is limited. Upon Fourier transform, the complex-valued signals exhibit the potential to resolve distinct frequency components with high spectral resolution. SLC SAR images hence demonstrate the potential to characterize electromagnetic scattering properties of ground objects and form high-dimensional feature representations to enhance discriminability. However, backscattered energy from different object instances are dispersed and overlapped in the frequency domain, making it challenging to isolate the spectral features of individual objects. This paper proposes a Spatial–Spectral Feature Guidance Network (SSFG-Net) that simultaneously leverages the nature of imagery and signal to form high-dimensional and discriminative features for the improvement of object detection and recognition. The core ideal is to construct a spatial–spectral feature cube, which extends single-dimensional intensity scalars into high-dimensional spectral features via spectral decomposition, forming discriminative features while preserving the spatial position information. Additionally, a Visual Feature Extraction Module (VFE) is designed to complement delicate visual features and model object instances with 2-dimensional Gaussian distributions for the holistic perception of object instances. Finally, a Correlation-Aware Feature Mixer (CAFM) is developed to compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between spatial–spectral and visual features, enabling efficient feature fusion based on the correlation analysis. Experiments on the FAIR-CSAR dataset show that SSFG-Net boosts average AP50 by 4.6% on spotlight mode and 9.1% on strip-map mode, compared to the baseline. The code is available at https://github.com/suoyuxi/SSFG .

item.title: AMTFusion: boosting 3D object detection by adaptive multi-modal temporal fusion and augmentation
item.dc:creator: Wei Tian
Zhenglin Du
Xiaolong Zhao
Qiankun Yu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Accurate perception of surrounding 3D objects is indispensable for ensuring the safety of autonomous driving systems. Furthermore, the integration of multi-modal sensors has emerged as a pivotal research area within detection paradigms based on Bird’s Eye View. In this paper, we introduce a sparse query-based 3D object detection framework by adaptive multi-modal temporal fusion of camera and LiDAR sensor data. Initially, we leverage the spatial-aware cross-modal feature aggregation to adaptively fuse instance-level point cloud and image features. We further elaborate a sequential temporal modeling approach, which facilitates an enhanced multi-modal 3D object detection within constant spatial and temporal complexity. To mitigate the challenge posed by limited high-quality training data, we incorporate a fragment-wise multi-modal temporal data augmentation strategy, aimed at bolstering the model’s performance for detecting long-tail distributed objects. Our approach achieves an NDS detection score of 75.5% on the nuScenes test set (without test time augmentation and model ensembling). These results underscore the efficacy of our approach and highlight its superiority compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. We further show that our approach remarkably improves the accuracy of multi-object tracking on nuScenes dataset. Published: 2026-03-31T19:54:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.831 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Tian; Zhenglin Du; Xiaolong Zhao; Qiankun Yu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3679483

Relevance: 0.831 (must_read)

Accurate perception of surrounding 3D objects is indispensable for ensuring the safety of autonomous driving systems. Furthermore, the integration of multi-modal sensors has emerged as a pivotal research area within detection paradigms based on Bird’s Eye View. In this paper, we introduce a sparse query-based 3D object detection framework by adaptive multi-modal temporal fusion of camera and LiDAR sensor data. Initially, we leverage the spatial-aware cross-modal feature aggregation to adaptively fuse instance-level point cloud and image features. We further elaborate a sequential temporal modeling approach, which facilitates an enhanced multi-modal 3D object detection within constant spatial and temporal complexity. To mitigate the challenge posed by limited high-quality training data, we incorporate a fragment-wise multi-modal temporal data augmentation strategy, aimed at bolstering the model’s performance for detecting long-tail distributed objects. Our approach achieves an NDS detection score of 75.5% on the nuScenes test set (without test time augmentation and model ensembling). These results underscore the efficacy of our approach and highlight its superiority compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. We further show that our approach remarkably improves the accuracy of multi-object tracking on nuScenes dataset.

item.title: A Semantic Representation Learning Perspective-based Approach for Multimodal Science Question Answering
item.dc:creator: Ling Gao
Nan Sheng
Rui Song
Hao Xu
item.description: Multimodal science question answering performs multimodal learning and logical reasoning based on given visual and text input to answer science questions. For a particular input pair, visual and text express the common semantic meaning through different modal carriers. The semantic meanings expressed by the visual and text modal carriers sourcing different pairs are independent. However, the high entanglement between modal and semantic representation and the weak semantic differences between different pairs hinder the learning of visual and text semantic representation. In this work, we introduce a semantic representation learning perspective-based approach (SRLP) for multimodal science question answering. First, we maximize the matching degree between visual and text semantic representations within the particular pair by disentanglement and agreement constraints. At the same time, we combine contrastive learning with disentangled representation learning to enhance the cross-modal semantic representation learning of the particular pair. The learned cross-modal semantic representation is finally fed into the decoder to generate the answer text. Experiments on two multimodal science question answering datasets demonstrate the excellent performance of SRLP, proving the effectiveness of cross-modal agreement and contrastive learning. Data and source code are available at https://github.com/gaoling-code/SRLP . Published: 2026-04-01T08:05:16+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.824 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ling Gao; Nan Sheng; Rui Song; Hao Xu

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104338

Relevance: 0.824 (must_read)

Multimodal science question answering performs multimodal learning and logical reasoning based on given visual and text input to answer science questions. For a particular input pair, visual and text express the common semantic meaning through different modal carriers. The semantic meanings expressed by the visual and text modal carriers sourcing different pairs are independent. However, the high entanglement between modal and semantic representation and the weak semantic differences between different pairs hinder the learning of visual and text semantic representation. In this work, we introduce a semantic representation learning perspective-based approach (SRLP) for multimodal science question answering. First, we maximize the matching degree between visual and text semantic representations within the particular pair by disentanglement and agreement constraints. At the same time, we combine contrastive learning with disentangled representation learning to enhance the cross-modal semantic representation learning of the particular pair. The learned cross-modal semantic representation is finally fed into the decoder to generate the answer text. Experiments on two multimodal science question answering datasets demonstrate the excellent performance of SRLP, proving the effectiveness of cross-modal agreement and contrastive learning. Data and source code are available at https://github.com/gaoling-code/SRLP .

item.title: TimeSenCLIP: A time series vision–language model for remote sensing
item.dc:creator: Pallavi Jain
Diego Marcos
Dino Ienco
Roberto Interdonato
Tristan Berchoux
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Vision–language models (VLMs) have shown significant promise in remote sensing applications, particularly for land-use and land-cover (LULC) mapping via zero-shot classification and retrieval. However, current approaches face several key challenges, such as the dependence on caption-based supervision, which is often not available or very limited in terms of the covered semantics, and the fact of being adapted from generic VLM architectures that are suitable for very high resolution images. Consequently, these models tend to prioritize spatial context over spectral and temporal information, limiting their effectiveness for medium-resolution remote sensing imagery. In this work, we present TimeSenCLIP, a lightweight VLM for remote sensing time series, using a cross-view temporal contrastive framework to align multispectral Sentinel-2 time series with geo-tagged ground-level imagery, without requiring textual annotations. Unlike prior VLMs, TimeSenCLIP emphasizes temporal and spectral signals over spatial context, investigating whether single-pixel time series contain sufficient information for solving a variety of tasks. Our approach is trained on the LUCAS and Sen4Map datasets and evaluated across four main mapping tasks: land cover, land use, habitat mapping and crop type classification. The CLIP text encoder can be used to probe the learned representations using semantically meaningful categories, enabling effective zero-shot generalization without task-specific text supervision. We further extend our evaluation to bioregions mapping and country-level image retrieval. Although coarse, these tasks are valuable for probing whether the model captures geographically meaningful representations, such as regional climate regimes, vegetation patterns, and land-use structures. TimeSenCLIP achieves consistently better performance than existing CLIP-based remote sensing models in both zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval. Notably, single-pixel multispectral time series variants remain highly competitive, particularly with extended temporal coverage, demonstrating that temporal–spectral dynamics can compensate to a substantial degree for the reduced spatial footprint. Published: 2026-03-31T13:43:17+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.819 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Pallavi Jain; Diego Marcos; Dino Ienco; Roberto Interdonato; Tristan Berchoux

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.043

Relevance: 0.819 (must_read)

Vision–language models (VLMs) have shown significant promise in remote sensing applications, particularly for land-use and land-cover (LULC) mapping via zero-shot classification and retrieval. However, current approaches face several key challenges, such as the dependence on caption-based supervision, which is often not available or very limited in terms of the covered semantics, and the fact of being adapted from generic VLM architectures that are suitable for very high resolution images. Consequently, these models tend to prioritize spatial context over spectral and temporal information, limiting their effectiveness for medium-resolution remote sensing imagery. In this work, we present TimeSenCLIP, a lightweight VLM for remote sensing time series, using a cross-view temporal contrastive framework to align multispectral Sentinel-2 time series with geo-tagged ground-level imagery, without requiring textual annotations. Unlike prior VLMs, TimeSenCLIP emphasizes temporal and spectral signals over spatial context, investigating whether single-pixel time series contain sufficient information for solving a variety of tasks. Our approach is trained on the LUCAS and Sen4Map datasets and evaluated across four main mapping tasks: land cover, land use, habitat mapping and crop type classification. The CLIP text encoder can be used to probe the learned representations using semantically meaningful categories, enabling effective zero-shot generalization without task-specific text supervision. We further extend our evaluation to bioregions mapping and country-level image retrieval. Although coarse, these tasks are valuable for probing whether the model captures geographically meaningful representations, such as regional climate regimes, vegetation patterns, and land-use structures. TimeSenCLIP achieves consistently better performance than existing CLIP-based remote sensing models in both zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval. Notably, single-pixel multispectral time series variants remain highly competitive, particularly with extended temporal coverage, demonstrating that temporal–spectral dynamics can compensate to a substantial degree for the reduced spatial footprint.

item.title: 红外空中弱小运动目标检测方法研究综述
item.dc:creator: LIU Yuhan
MA Yapeng
YANG Jianwei
WANG Ziming
AERSI Aliha
CAO Huazhao
WEN Zixiao
HU Shengran
ZHOU Guangyao
HU Yuxin
item.description: 红外搜索与跟踪系统主要以红外成像探测为核心环节，凭借其全天时成像、部署灵活、易于隐蔽等优势，在军事与民用领域得到广泛应用。近年来，随着军事技术的快速发展，红外搜索与跟踪系统在预警侦察、安防监控及导弹制导等方面发挥着重要作用。然而受红外传感器性能、成像距离远、环境复杂多变等因素影响，观测目标往往呈现为可探测性极低的弱小目标形态，且伴随灵活的运动状态，导致核心的红外弱小运动目标检测极具挑战。本文根据检测方法原理，将近年来的检测方法归纳为三类：基于传统检测框架的方法、基于低秩稀疏分解框架的方法以及基于深度学习框架的方法，并对这些方法进行综述分析。同时，介绍相关数据，开展多种方法的对比实验与性能分析，最后对红外弱小运动目标检测方法进行总结与展望，旨在为学者们提供参考帮助。（相关参考资料可见：https：//github.com/Yoooohan/Collection-for-Infrared-dim-and-small-target-detection-methods以及https：//www.scidb.cn/preview？dataSetId=c155704e0ff14de1af739528423ce031&version=V1） Published: 2026-03-31T07:06:45+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.815 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: LIU Yuhan; MA Yapeng; YANG Jianwei; WANG Ziming; AERSI Aliha; CAO Huazhao; WEN Zixiao; HU Shengran; ZHOU Guangyao; HU Yuxin

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.260109

Relevance: 0.815 (must_read)

红外搜索与跟踪系统主要以红外成像探测为核心环节，凭借其全天时成像、部署灵活、易于隐蔽等优势，在军事与民用领域得到广泛应用。近年来，随着军事技术的快速发展，红外搜索与跟踪系统在预警侦察、安防监控及导弹制导等方面发挥着重要作用。然而受红外传感器性能、成像距离远、环境复杂多变等因素影响，观测目标往往呈现为可探测性极低的弱小目标形态，且伴随灵活的运动状态，导致核心的红外弱小运动目标检测极具挑战。本文根据检测方法原理，将近年来的检测方法归纳为三类：基于传统检测框架的方法、基于低秩稀疏分解框架的方法以及基于深度学习框架的方法，并对这些方法进行综述分析。同时，介绍相关数据，开展多种方法的对比实验与性能分析，最后对红外弱小运动目标检测方法进行总结与展望，旨在为学者们提供参考帮助。（相关参考资料可见：https：//github.com/Yoooohan/Collection-for-Infrared-dim-and-small-target-detection-methods以及https：//www.scidb.cn/preview？dataSetId=c155704e0ff14de1af739528423ce031&version=V1）

item.title: Dual Adversarial Perturbations for Zero-Shot Learning
item.dc:creator: Lei Wang
Shiming Chen
Guo-Sen Xie
Chaojian Yu
Xinhua You
Qinmu Peng
Xinge You
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: In Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL), embedding-based methods learn a visual–semantic mapping that leverages the attribute knowledge of seen classes to predict the attributes of unseen classes, enabling knowledge transfer from seen to unseen classes. However, distributional discrepancies between seen and unseen classes introduce an inherent domain shift, and inter-class variations cause the same attribute to be expressed differently across categories. As a result, models trained on seen classes often struggle to accurately recognize attributes in unseen classes, limiting their generalization ability. To address these challenges, we propose DAPZSL, a dual adversarial perturbation framework that enhances the robustness of visual–semantic mappings through Feature-Level Adversarial Perturbation (FAP) and improves the model’s generalization ability via Weight-Level Adversarial Perturbation (WAP). Specifically, FAP generates semantically perturbed adversarial samples at the feature level, and incorporating these samples during training encourages the model to learn more robust visual–semantic mappings that are resilient to semantic variations, which improves attribute recognition on unseen classes. Meanwhile, WAP introduces adversarial perturbations into the model’s weight space, promoting a flatter loss landscape that alleviates overfitting to seen classes and enhances generalization. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets—including AWA2, SUN, and CUB—demonstrate that DAPZSL significantly outperforms existing ZSL models. Published: 2026-04-01T20:14:32+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.811 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lei Wang; Shiming Chen; Guo-Sen Xie; Chaojian Yu; Xinhua You; Qinmu Peng; Xinge You

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3679600

Relevance: 0.811 (must_read)

In Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL), embedding-based methods learn a visual–semantic mapping that leverages the attribute knowledge of seen classes to predict the attributes of unseen classes, enabling knowledge transfer from seen to unseen classes. However, distributional discrepancies between seen and unseen classes introduce an inherent domain shift, and inter-class variations cause the same attribute to be expressed differently across categories. As a result, models trained on seen classes often struggle to accurately recognize attributes in unseen classes, limiting their generalization ability. To address these challenges, we propose DAPZSL, a dual adversarial perturbation framework that enhances the robustness of visual–semantic mappings through Feature-Level Adversarial Perturbation (FAP) and improves the model’s generalization ability via Weight-Level Adversarial Perturbation (WAP). Specifically, FAP generates semantically perturbed adversarial samples at the feature level, and incorporating these samples during training encourages the model to learn more robust visual–semantic mappings that are resilient to semantic variations, which improves attribute recognition on unseen classes. Meanwhile, WAP introduces adversarial perturbations into the model’s weight space, promoting a flatter loss landscape that alleviates overfitting to seen classes and enhances generalization. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets—including AWA2, SUN, and CUB—demonstrate that DAPZSL significantly outperforms existing ZSL models.

item.title: SARCapsNet: A Capsule Network for High-Accuracy SAR Automatic Target Recognition Under Limited Training Sample Conditions
item.dc:creator: Zhiyuan Liao
Chunquan Li
Junjie Zeng
Junzhi Yu
Yachao Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: Most existing synthetic aperture radar (SAR) automatic target recognition (ATR) methods perform well with large-scale training data, but their performance significantly decreases in small-sample scenarios. Therefore, developing a SAR ATR method that can provide high accuracy and lightweight performance under different training sample sizes is a highly challenging problem. To address this problem, this paper pro poses a capsule network, named SARCapsNet, which consists of a hierarchical adaptive threshold capsule encoder (HATCE) and a reconstruction decoder. Particularly, HATCE is the most significant innovation of the proposed SARCapsNet. Specifically, in the proposed HATCE, an adaptive deformable threshold module (ADTM) is first developed by rationally fusing deformable convolution and soft threshold denoising techniques. This ADTM can adaptively capture the complex morphological features and key scattering distribution of SAR targets, while mitigating the impact of speckle noise. Second, different from ADTM, an adaptive thresholding module (ATM) is developed by reasonably integrating standard convolution with soft threshold denoising so that such ATM can further learn morphology-stable high-level features and enhance the model's ability to capture more robust and generalized structural features. Furthermore, a shuffle attention mechanism is incorporated into the HATCE to adaptively emphasize important features by capturing fine-grained, pixel level pairwise relationships and inter-channel dependencies, while suppressing irrelevant information. By ingeniously integrating ADTM, ATM, and shuffle attention mechanisms into HATCE, SARCapsNet can not only provide excellent recognition accuracy under different numbers of training samples but also exhibit lightweight characteristics. The experimental results show that SARCapsNet not only outperforms state-of-the-art models under both limited and full training sample conditions, but also maintains its lightweight characteri... Published: 2026-04-01T20:14:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyuan Liao; Chunquan Li; Junjie Zeng; Junzhi Yu; Yachao Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3679848

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Most existing synthetic aperture radar (SAR) automatic target recognition (ATR) methods perform well with large-scale training data, but their performance significantly decreases in small-sample scenarios. Therefore, developing a SAR ATR method that can provide high accuracy and lightweight performance under different training sample sizes is a highly challenging problem. To address this problem, this paper pro poses a capsule network, named SARCapsNet, which consists of a hierarchical adaptive threshold capsule encoder (HATCE) and a reconstruction decoder. Particularly, HATCE is the most significant innovation of the proposed SARCapsNet. Specifically, in the proposed HATCE, an adaptive deformable threshold module (ADTM) is first developed by rationally fusing deformable convolution and soft threshold denoising techniques. This ADTM can adaptively capture the complex morphological features and key scattering distribution of SAR targets, while mitigating the impact of speckle noise. Second, different from ADTM, an adaptive thresholding module (ATM) is developed by reasonably integrating standard convolution with soft threshold denoising so that such ATM can further learn morphology-stable high-level features and enhance the model's ability to capture more robust and generalized structural features. Furthermore, a shuffle attention mechanism is incorporated into the HATCE to adaptively emphasize important features by capturing fine-grained, pixel level pairwise relationships and inter-channel dependencies, while suppressing irrelevant information. By ingeniously integrating ADTM, ATM, and shuffle attention mechanisms into HATCE, SARCapsNet can not only provide excellent recognition accuracy under different numbers of training samples but also exhibit lightweight characteristics. The experimental results show that SARCapsNet not only outperforms state-of-the-art models under both limited and full training sample conditions, but also maintains its lightweight characteri...

item.title: Learning 3D Representation from Auto-labeled 2D Object Boxes
item.dc:creator: Qian Deng
Le Hui
Jian Yang
Jin Xie
item.description: Recent advances in LiDAR representation learning with limited annotations show strong promise. Existing well-performed methods mainly focus on distilling the 2D representation into the 3D representation via superpixels. Superpixels are used to construct the cross-modal contrastive learning, leading to semantic ambiguity of 3D features belonging to the same object and impairing the performance. To this end, we aim to leverage unlabeled LiDAR-camera pairs to design a novel pre-training pipeline, which learns from category space directly and pulls the 3D features belonging to the same object close. Specifically, we obtain autolabeled 2D object boxes with a fixed 2D open-vocabulary object detector and transform the labeled 2D object boxes into high-quality pixel-wise label maps with a box-to-label-maps generation algorithm. Based on the pseudo labels, we present a dual-space pre-training 3D network that recognizes accurate categories from the semantic priors of paired 3D points and segments complete objects. Furthermore, we propose a module named AdaptPro to improve performance further when fine-tuning the 3D network under limited annotations, aiming to explore the unpaired 3D features that lack 2D correspondences via category prototypes. The experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performances on both the nuScenes and SemanticKITTI benchmark datasets. Code is avialable at https://github.com/dengq7/Box4Scene. Published: 2026-03-31T19:54:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qian Deng; Le Hui; Jian Yang; Jin Xie

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3671587

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Recent advances in LiDAR representation learning with limited annotations show strong promise. Existing well-performed methods mainly focus on distilling the 2D representation into the 3D representation via superpixels. Superpixels are used to construct the cross-modal contrastive learning, leading to semantic ambiguity of 3D features belonging to the same object and impairing the performance. To this end, we aim to leverage unlabeled LiDAR-camera pairs to design a novel pre-training pipeline, which learns from category space directly and pulls the 3D features belonging to the same object close. Specifically, we obtain autolabeled 2D object boxes with a fixed 2D open-vocabulary object detector and transform the labeled 2D object boxes into high-quality pixel-wise label maps with a box-to-label-maps generation algorithm. Based on the pseudo labels, we present a dual-space pre-training 3D network that recognizes accurate categories from the semantic priors of paired 3D points and segments complete objects. Furthermore, we propose a module named AdaptPro to improve performance further when fine-tuning the 3D network under limited annotations, aiming to explore the unpaired 3D features that lack 2D correspondences via category prototypes. The experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performances on both the nuScenes and SemanticKITTI benchmark datasets. Code is avialable at https://github.com/dengq7/Box4Scene.

item.title: Chat-Scene++: Exploiting Context-Rich Object Identification for 3D LLM
item.dc:creator: Haifeng Huang
Yilun Chen
Zehan Wang
Jiangmiao Pang
Zhou Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Recent advancements in multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for 3D scene understanding. However, existing methods struggle with fine-grained object grounding and contextual reasoning, limiting their ability to interpret and interact with complex 3D environments. In this paper, we present Chat-Scene++, an MLLM framework that represents 3D scenes as context-rich object sequences. By structuring scenes as sequences of objects with contextual semantics, Chat-Scene++ enables object-centric representation and interaction. It decomposes a 3D scene into object representations paired with identifier tokens, allowing LLMs to follow instructions across diverse 3D vision-language tasks. To capture inter-object relationships and global semantics, Chat-Scene++ extracts context-rich object features using large-scale pre-trained 3D scene-level and 2D image-level encoders, unlike the isolated per-object features in Chat-Scene. Its flexible object-centric design also supports grounded chain-of-thought (G-CoT) reasoning, enabling the model to distinguish objects at both category and spatial levels during multi-step inference. Without the need for additional task-specific heads or fine-tuning, Chat-Scene++ achieves state-of-the-art performance on five major 3D vision-language benchmarks: ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer, Scan2Cap, ScanQA, and SQA3D. These results highlight its effectiveness in scene comprehension, object grounding, and spatial reasoning. Additionally, without reconstructing 3D worlds through computationally expensive processes, we demonstrate its applicability to real-world scenarios using only 2D inputs. Code will be made available at https://github.com/ZzZZCHS/Chat-Scene https://github.com/ZzZZCHS/Chat-Scene. Published: 2026-03-31T19:52:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haifeng Huang; Yilun Chen; Zehan Wang; Jiangmiao Pang; Zhou Zhao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3679561

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Recent advancements in multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for 3D scene understanding. However, existing methods struggle with fine-grained object grounding and contextual reasoning, limiting their ability to interpret and interact with complex 3D environments. In this paper, we present Chat-Scene++, an MLLM framework that represents 3D scenes as context-rich object sequences. By structuring scenes as sequences of objects with contextual semantics, Chat-Scene++ enables object-centric representation and interaction. It decomposes a 3D scene into object representations paired with identifier tokens, allowing LLMs to follow instructions across diverse 3D vision-language tasks. To capture inter-object relationships and global semantics, Chat-Scene++ extracts context-rich object features using large-scale pre-trained 3D scene-level and 2D image-level encoders, unlike the isolated per-object features in Chat-Scene. Its flexible object-centric design also supports grounded chain-of-thought (G-CoT) reasoning, enabling the model to distinguish objects at both category and spatial levels during multi-step inference. Without the need for additional task-specific heads or fine-tuning, Chat-Scene++ achieves state-of-the-art performance on five major 3D vision-language benchmarks: ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer, Scan2Cap, ScanQA, and SQA3D. These results highlight its effectiveness in scene comprehension, object grounding, and spatial reasoning. Additionally, without reconstructing 3D worlds through computationally expensive processes, we demonstrate its applicability to real-world scenarios using only 2D inputs. Code will be made available at https://github.com/ZzZZCHS/Chat-Scene https://github.com/ZzZZCHS/Chat-Scene.

item.title: Match Stereo Videos Via Bidirectional Alignment
item.dc:creator: Junpeng Jing
Ye Mao
Anlan Qiu
Krystian Mikolajczyk
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Video stereo matching is the task of estimating consistent disparity maps from rectified stereo videos. There is considerable scope for improvement in both datasets and methods within this area. Recent learning-based methods often focus on optimizing performance for independent stereo pairs, leading to temporal inconsistencies in videos. Existing video methods typically employ sliding window operation over time dimension, which can result in low-frequency oscillations corresponding to the window size. To address these challenges, we propose a bidirectional alignment mechanism for adjacent frames as a fundamental operation. Building on this, we introduce a novel video processing framework, BiDAStereo, and a plugin stabilizer network, BiDAStabilizer, compatible with general image-based methods. Regarding datasets, current synthetic object-based and indoor datasets are commonly used for training and benchmarking, with a lack of outdoor nature scenarios. To bridge this gap, we present a realistic synthetic dataset and benchmark focused on natural scenes, along with a real-world dataset captured by a stereo camera in diverse urban scenes for qualitative evaluation. Extensive experiments on in-domain, out-of-domain, and robustness evaluation demonstrate the contribution of our methods and datasets, showcasing improvements in prediction quality and achieving state-of-the-art results on various commonly used benchmarks. The project page, demos, code, and datasets are available at: https://tomtomtommi.github.io/BiDAVideo/. Published: 2026-03-31T19:52:44+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.809 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3679033

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Video stereo matching is the task of estimating consistent disparity maps from rectified stereo videos. There is considerable scope for improvement in both datasets and methods within this area. Recent learning-based methods often focus on optimizing performance for independent stereo pairs, leading to temporal inconsistencies in videos. Existing video methods typically employ sliding window operation over time dimension, which can result in low-frequency oscillations corresponding to the window size. To address these challenges, we propose a bidirectional alignment mechanism for adjacent frames as a fundamental operation. Building on this, we introduce a novel video processing framework, BiDAStereo, and a plugin stabilizer network, BiDAStabilizer, compatible with general image-based methods. Regarding datasets, current synthetic object-based and indoor datasets are commonly used for training and benchmarking, with a lack of outdoor nature scenarios. To bridge this gap, we present a realistic synthetic dataset and benchmark focused on natural scenes, along with a real-world dataset captured by a stereo camera in diverse urban scenes for qualitative evaluation. Extensive experiments on in-domain, out-of-domain, and robustness evaluation demonstrate the contribution of our methods and datasets, showcasing improvements in prediction quality and achieving state-of-the-art results on various commonly used benchmarks. The project page, demos, code, and datasets are available at: https://tomtomtommi.github.io/BiDAVideo/.

item.title: Adaptive Fusion Image Enhancement and Refinement via ERnet for Robust Object Detection under Adverse Weather
item.dc:creator: Zhiyong Jing
Zhaobing Chen
Lei Shi
Yufei Gao
Yucheng Shi
Lin Wei
Xiangjie Wang
Qingxian Wang
item.description: Vehicle detection is a critical component of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), but it faces challenges in real-world traffic scenarios due to complex and ever-changing weather conditions such as rain, snow, fog, sandstorms, and dust. These conditions can significantly degrade image quality and reduce the accuracy of vehicle detection. To address these issues, we propose an enhancement and refinement fusion framework called ERnet, which adaptively enhances and refines each image to improve visibility and detection performance. ERnet consists of two modules: the Adaptive Digital Image Enhancement (ADIE) module and the feature refinement (RefineNet) module. The ADIE module enhances images captured under adverse weather conditions, while the RefineNet module focuses on refining image details. Multiple loss functions are incorporated to constrain RefineNet and improve visualization and feature details. We conducted experiments to compare ERnet with previous methods in various real traffic scenarios. The results demonstrate that ERnet effectively enhances images obtained under both normal and adverse weather conditions. Specifically, under adverse weather conditions, ERnet improves the mean average precision (mAP) by 6.17%. Furthermore, it achieves improvements of 7.57%, 3.12%, 6.74%, and 3.45% in foggy, rainy, sandy, and snowy conditions, respectively. Notably, even in sunny weather, ERnet only reduces the mAP by 0.48%, indicating its effectiveness and robustness in various weather conditions. The findings highlight the potential of ERnet to provide higher environmental applicability to ITS and offer solution ideas for ITS applications in different weather conditions through fusion-based enhancement and refinement strategies. Published: 2026-04-01T15:38:51+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhiyong Jing; Zhaobing Chen; Lei Shi; Yufei Gao; Yucheng Shi; Lin Wei; Xiangjie Wang; Qingxian Wang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104342

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Vehicle detection is a critical component of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), but it faces challenges in real-world traffic scenarios due to complex and ever-changing weather conditions such as rain, snow, fog, sandstorms, and dust. These conditions can significantly degrade image quality and reduce the accuracy of vehicle detection. To address these issues, we propose an enhancement and refinement fusion framework called ERnet, which adaptively enhances and refines each image to improve visibility and detection performance. ERnet consists of two modules: the Adaptive Digital Image Enhancement (ADIE) module and the feature refinement (RefineNet) module. The ADIE module enhances images captured under adverse weather conditions, while the RefineNet module focuses on refining image details. Multiple loss functions are incorporated to constrain RefineNet and improve visualization and feature details. We conducted experiments to compare ERnet with previous methods in various real traffic scenarios. The results demonstrate that ERnet effectively enhances images obtained under both normal and adverse weather conditions. Specifically, under adverse weather conditions, ERnet improves the mean average precision (mAP) by 6.17%. Furthermore, it achieves improvements of 7.57%, 3.12%, 6.74%, and 3.45% in foggy, rainy, sandy, and snowy conditions, respectively. Notably, even in sunny weather, ERnet only reduces the mAP by 0.48%, indicating its effectiveness and robustness in various weather conditions. The findings highlight the potential of ERnet to provide higher environmental applicability to ITS and offer solution ideas for ITS applications in different weather conditions through fusion-based enhancement and refinement strategies.

item.title: FTransMamba: A Multi-Stage Fusion Transformer and Mamba Modeling for Multimodal Remote Sensing Scene Understanding
item.dc:creator: Zhengpeng Li
Jun Hu
Weichun Guan
Jiawei Miao
Kunyang Wu
Zhiguo Xia
Bin Yang
Jiansheng Wu
item.description: To address intrinsic challenges in multimodal remote sensing scene understanding, such as modality-dependent feature discrepancy, cross-modal misalignment, and scale-dependent modality reliability, as well as the limitations of existing multimodal methods in modality discrepancy modeling, spatial alignment, and multi-scale feature fusion, this study proposes a novel multi-stage cross-modal fusion network, named FTransMamba. The architecture follows an encoder–decoder design and employs a progressive fusion strategy to effectively integrate RGB imagery with auxiliary data sources. A dual-branch structure is used to extract multi-scale features from both RGB and auxiliary inputs in parallel. At each scale, the bidirectional orthogonal spatial attention (BOSA) module dynamically aligns channel and spatial information across modalities. To enhance global semantic consistency, the transformer encoder (TE) module models joint spectral–spatial attention. The bidirectional mamba (Bi-Mamba) module mitigates directional bias introduced by one-way processing by incorporating information from both forward and backward passes, thereby improving global context aggregation and cross-modal feature integration. Finally, the adaptive cascaded receptive field (ACRF) module fuses multi-scale semantic information while preserving fine-grained details from earlier layers. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Vaihingen, Potsdam, and WHU-OPT-SAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of FTransMamba. The model achieves mIoU scores of approximately 84.95%, 87.63%, and 67.88%, respectively, along with consistent improvements in mF1. Compared to existing approaches, FTransMamba delivers superior segmentation accuracy and robustness across diverse scenes. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/lzp-lkd/FTransMamba . Published: 2026-03-31T06:44:25+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhengpeng Li; Jun Hu; Weichun Guan; Jiawei Miao; Kunyang Wu; Zhiguo Xia; Bin Yang; Jiansheng Wu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113625

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

To address intrinsic challenges in multimodal remote sensing scene understanding, such as modality-dependent feature discrepancy, cross-modal misalignment, and scale-dependent modality reliability, as well as the limitations of existing multimodal methods in modality discrepancy modeling, spatial alignment, and multi-scale feature fusion, this study proposes a novel multi-stage cross-modal fusion network, named FTransMamba. The architecture follows an encoder–decoder design and employs a progressive fusion strategy to effectively integrate RGB imagery with auxiliary data sources. A dual-branch structure is used to extract multi-scale features from both RGB and auxiliary inputs in parallel. At each scale, the bidirectional orthogonal spatial attention (BOSA) module dynamically aligns channel and spatial information across modalities. To enhance global semantic consistency, the transformer encoder (TE) module models joint spectral–spatial attention. The bidirectional mamba (Bi-Mamba) module mitigates directional bias introduced by one-way processing by incorporating information from both forward and backward passes, thereby improving global context aggregation and cross-modal feature integration. Finally, the adaptive cascaded receptive field (ACRF) module fuses multi-scale semantic information while preserving fine-grained details from earlier layers. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Vaihingen, Potsdam, and WHU-OPT-SAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of FTransMamba. The model achieves mIoU scores of approximately 84.95%, 87.63%, and 67.88%, respectively, along with consistent improvements in mF1. Compared to existing approaches, FTransMamba delivers superior segmentation accuracy and robustness across diverse scenes. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/lzp-lkd/FTransMamba .

item.title: Distribution-Aware Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Models with Dynamic Boundary Prototype
item.dc:creator: Xi Yang
Xinyue Zhong
Nannan Wang
item.description: Prompt learning has emerged as an effective strategy for adapting vision-language models (VLMs) which injects learnable semantic prompts into VLMs to guide the alignment between visual and textual representations. Although existing methods have shown strong performance across various tasks, they usually focus on the representative class-level samples and overlook the atypical and hard samples in visual feature space, which hinders generalization of VLMs. To address this issue, we propose the concept of dynamic boundary prototype, which highlights ambiguous samples that are far from the class centroid and is updated at each epoch. Accordingly, we propose a Distribution-Aware Prompt Learning (DAPL) framework to calibrate the distribution of visual feature space via the definition, optimization, and updating of dynamic boundary prototypes. Firstly, we introduce Boundary-Centroid Pulling to optimize the intra-class distribution by progressively reducing the distance between boundary and centroid prototypes, thereby enhancing structural consistency within each class. Secondly, to further enhance inter-class separability, a distance-weighted contrastive loss that places greater emphasis on distinguishing adjacent classes is designed, facilitating more effective fine-grained discrimination. Thirdly, we apply Low-Rank Adaptation Fine-Tuning to adapt the vision encoder through targeted modifications to its self-attention layers. Additionally, we adopt a progressive training strategy for stable optimization. DAPL is compatible with mainstream prompt learning methods such as CoOp, CoCoOp and PromptKD, and consistently improves their average performance across 11 benchmark datasets. Published: 2026-04-01T20:15:01+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xi Yang; Xinyue Zhong; Nannan Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3678014

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Prompt learning has emerged as an effective strategy for adapting vision-language models (VLMs) which injects learnable semantic prompts into VLMs to guide the alignment between visual and textual representations. Although existing methods have shown strong performance across various tasks, they usually focus on the representative class-level samples and overlook the atypical and hard samples in visual feature space, which hinders generalization of VLMs. To address this issue, we propose the concept of dynamic boundary prototype, which highlights ambiguous samples that are far from the class centroid and is updated at each epoch. Accordingly, we propose a Distribution-Aware Prompt Learning (DAPL) framework to calibrate the distribution of visual feature space via the definition, optimization, and updating of dynamic boundary prototypes. Firstly, we introduce Boundary-Centroid Pulling to optimize the intra-class distribution by progressively reducing the distance between boundary and centroid prototypes, thereby enhancing structural consistency within each class. Secondly, to further enhance inter-class separability, a distance-weighted contrastive loss that places greater emphasis on distinguishing adjacent classes is designed, facilitating more effective fine-grained discrimination. Thirdly, we apply Low-Rank Adaptation Fine-Tuning to adapt the vision encoder through targeted modifications to its self-attention layers. Additionally, we adopt a progressive training strategy for stable optimization. DAPL is compatible with mainstream prompt learning methods such as CoOp, CoCoOp and PromptKD, and consistently improves their average performance across 11 benchmark datasets.

item.title: 面向天基监视的红外弱小目标检测与跟踪仿真数据集IRSatVideo
item.dc:creator: Ying Xinyi
Zhang Xin
Zhou Xiang
Cao Xu
Lin Zaipin
Li Miao
Zhou Shilin
An Wei
item.description: 天基红外序列图像中的弱小目标检测与跟踪技术在空间态势感知等领域具有重要应用价值。然而，该领域的发展长期受限于真实数据获取困难、现有数据集规模小、场景单一且缺乏物理真实性的挑战。本文旨在构建一个大规模、高保真、涵盖多样轨道场景的红外小目标序列数据集，以促进相关算法的研究与发展。具体而言，本文提出一种半仿真的数据构建方法，利用Landsat和高分四号卫星的真实红外影像作为背景，通过精确建模卫星平台运动（姿态、轨道）、目标特性（形态、轨迹、辐射强度）以及复杂的背景杂波，生成了包含低地球轨道（LEO）和地球同步轨道（GEO）两种场景的大规模序列数据集IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO。IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO数据集分别包含200/200段序列、220126/344285个目标实例，并提供实例级像素掩码和矩形框标注。实验显示，在IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO数据集上，典型基线算法可实现20.38/33.70的IoU、41.05/73.39的检测率与8.56E-8/6.31E-6的虚警率。IRSatVideo是首个专为天基红外弱小目标检测与跟踪设计的大规模仿真数据集，其创新性体现在三个方面：动态模拟卫星平台运动引起的背景变化；涵盖从机动目标到信杂比低至1的极弱目标；包含多红外波段及全球多样背景。通过对目标尺寸、信杂比、运动特性及背景复杂度等关键属性的统计分析，并与现有数据集对比，表明IRSatVideo在规模、多样性和物理真实性方面具有显著优势。本数据集为天基红外弱小目标检测研究提供了一个接近真实、挑战性强的基准平台，有望显著加速相关算法的研发与评测进程，对推动空间态势感知技术进步具有重要意义。数据集下载链接：https：//github.com/XinyiYing/IRSatVideo Published: 2026-03-31T07:06:45+00:00 Venue: Journal of Image and Graphics Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ying Xinyi; Zhang Xin; Zhou Xiang; Cao Xu; Lin Zaipin; Li Miao; Zhou Shilin; An Wei

Journal: Journal of Image and Graphics

DOI: 10.11834/jig.250617

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

天基红外序列图像中的弱小目标检测与跟踪技术在空间态势感知等领域具有重要应用价值。然而，该领域的发展长期受限于真实数据获取困难、现有数据集规模小、场景单一且缺乏物理真实性的挑战。本文旨在构建一个大规模、高保真、涵盖多样轨道场景的红外小目标序列数据集，以促进相关算法的研究与发展。具体而言，本文提出一种半仿真的数据构建方法，利用Landsat和高分四号卫星的真实红外影像作为背景，通过精确建模卫星平台运动（姿态、轨道）、目标特性（形态、轨迹、辐射强度）以及复杂的背景杂波，生成了包含低地球轨道（LEO）和地球同步轨道（GEO）两种场景的大规模序列数据集IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO。IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO数据集分别包含200/200段序列、220126/344285个目标实例，并提供实例级像素掩码和矩形框标注。实验显示，在IRSatVideo-LEO与IRSatVideo-GEO数据集上，典型基线算法可实现20.38/33.70的IoU、41.05/73.39的检测率与8.56E-8/6.31E-6的虚警率。IRSatVideo是首个专为天基红外弱小目标检测与跟踪设计的大规模仿真数据集，其创新性体现在三个方面：动态模拟卫星平台运动引起的背景变化；涵盖从机动目标到信杂比低至1的极弱目标；包含多红外波段及全球多样背景。通过对目标尺寸、信杂比、运动特性及背景复杂度等关键属性的统计分析，并与现有数据集对比，表明IRSatVideo在规模、多样性和物理真实性方面具有显著优势。本数据集为天基红外弱小目标检测研究提供了一个接近真实、挑战性强的基准平台，有望显著加速相关算法的研发与评测进程，对推动空间态势感知技术进步具有重要意义。数据集下载链接：https：//github.com/XinyiYing/IRSatVideo

item.title: Self-supervised pre-training enables marine debris detection across sensors
item.dc:creator: Emanuele Dalsasso
Marc Rußwurm
Christian Donner
Samuel Darmon
Robin de Vries
Michele Volpi
Devis Tuia
item.description: Plastic pollution is a global crisis that needs to be addressed at scale. Current efforts have focused on annotating Sentinel-2 acquisitions for floating debris, and machine learning models are trained to automatically detect such marine debris. However, Sentinel-2 has a revisit time of approximately one week, and images are acquired at 10m resolution, leading to insufficient spatial and temporal resolution for collection efforts. To pave the way for daily monitoring of marine pollution at high spatial and temporal resolution, we propose a cross-sensor approach that allows to transfer the model’s knowledge from Sentinel-2 to daily-acquired PlanetScope images at 3m resolution. Being self-supervised, the proposed method does not require labels for PlanetScope data, therefore relieving the burden of creating a large-scale annotated set of PlanetScope images. Our approach shows its effectiveness over the Marine Debris Detector on a set of images that we annotated for validation purposes. We share the annotations and the model for benchmarking. Published: 2026-03-31T13:41:52+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.801 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Emanuele Dalsasso; Marc Rußwurm; Christian Donner; Samuel Darmon; Robin de Vries; Michele Volpi; Devis Tuia

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115391

Relevance: 0.801 (must_read)

Plastic pollution is a global crisis that needs to be addressed at scale. Current efforts have focused on annotating Sentinel-2 acquisitions for floating debris, and machine learning models are trained to automatically detect such marine debris. However, Sentinel-2 has a revisit time of approximately one week, and images are acquired at 10m resolution, leading to insufficient spatial and temporal resolution for collection efforts. To pave the way for daily monitoring of marine pollution at high spatial and temporal resolution, we propose a cross-sensor approach that allows to transfer the model’s knowledge from Sentinel-2 to daily-acquired PlanetScope images at 3m resolution. Being self-supervised, the proposed method does not require labels for PlanetScope data, therefore relieving the burden of creating a large-scale annotated set of PlanetScope images. Our approach shows its effectiveness over the Marine Debris Detector on a set of images that we annotated for validation purposes. We share the annotations and the model for benchmarking.

item.title: Predicting new research directions in materials science using large language models and concept graphs
item.dc:creator: Thomas Marwitz
Alexander Colsmann
Ben Breitung
Christoph Brabec
Christoph Kirchlechner
Eva Blasco
Gabriel Cadilha Marques
Horst Hahn
Michael Hirtz
Pavel A. Levkin
Yolita M. Eggeler
Tobias Schlöder
Pascal Friederich
item.description: Abstract Due to an exponential increase in published research articles, it is impossible for individual scientists to read all publications, even within their own research field. Here we investigate the use of large language models to extract the main concepts and semantic information from scientific abstracts in the domain of materials science to identify links that were not noticed by humans and to suggest inspiring near and/or mid-term future research directions. We show that large language models can extract concepts more efficiently than automated keyword extraction methods to build a concept graph as an abstraction of the scientific literature. A machine learning model is trained to predict emerging combinations of concepts, that is, new research ideas, based on historical data. We demonstrate that integrating semantic concept information leads to increased prediction performance. The applicability of our model is demonstrated in qualitative interviews with domain experts based on individualized model suggestions. We show that the model can inspire materials scientists in their creative thinking process by predicting innovative combinations of concepts that have not yet been investigated. Published: 2026-04-01T09:05:03+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.800 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Thomas Marwitz; Alexander Colsmann; Ben Breitung; Christoph Brabec; Christoph Kirchlechner; Eva Blasco; Gabriel Cadilha Marques; Horst Hahn; Michael Hirtz; Pavel A. Levkin; Yolita M. Eggeler; Tobias Schlöder; Pascal Friederich

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1038/s42256-026-01206-y

Relevance: 0.800 (must_read)

Abstract Due to an exponential increase in published research articles, it is impossible for individual scientists to read all publications, even within their own research field. Here we investigate the use of large language models to extract the main concepts and semantic information from scientific abstracts in the domain of materials science to identify links that were not noticed by humans and to suggest inspiring near and/or mid-term future research directions. We show that large language models can extract concepts more efficiently than automated keyword extraction methods to build a concept graph as an abstraction of the scientific literature. A machine learning model is trained to predict emerging combinations of concepts, that is, new research ideas, based on historical data. We demonstrate that integrating semantic concept information leads to increased prediction performance. The applicability of our model is demonstrated in qualitative interviews with domain experts based on individualized model suggestions. We show that the model can inspire materials scientists in their creative thinking process by predicting innovative combinations of concepts that have not yet been investigated.

item.title: Modality Fusion and Masked Complementary Modeling Based Pre-training for Brain Tumor Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Jianwen Liang
Li Lin
Junyan Lyu
Pujin Cheng
Xiaoying Tang
item.description: Self-supervised learning (SSL), which leverages large amounts of unlabeled data to reduce dependence on extensive annotations, has demonstrated remarkable transferability in medical image segmentation tasks. However, most existing SSL approaches for medical imaging are limited to single-modality data, overlooking the intrinsic complementarity among different imaging modalities. In brain tumor analysis, multimodal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) plays a crucial role in accurately delineating tumor subregions, as each modality provides distinct yet complementary information about tumor morphology and physiology. Therefore, effectively exploiting multimodal information during SSL has emerged as an important research focus for advancing brain tumor segmentation. In this study, we introduce a novel SSL framework, Modality Fusion and Masked Complementary Modeling (MF-MCM), developed for multimodal brain MRI. MF-MCM first performs modality-wise fusion through weighted sum of the input modalities to generate fused images equal in number to the input modalities. Subsequently, it applies complementary masking to the multichannel image constructed by concatenating the fused images, ensuring that the unmasked regions across all channels are non-overlapping and they jointly occupy the whole brain. Finally, by optimizing the multimodal image reconstruction and fusion coefficients regression tasks, the pre-training model is endowed with the ability to perceive fine-grained neuroanatomical details, integrate multimodal information, and achieve effective cross-modality interaction. The proposed SSL framework is fine-tuned and evaluated on four brain tumor segmentation datasets and one stroke lesion segmentation dataset. Results demonstrate that MF-MCM outperforms various state-of-the-art medical SSL methods while effectively reducing annotation requirements and maintaining robustness in scenarios where certain modalities are absent. Codes and pre-trained models will be available at https://github.com/liangjianwen01/MF-MCM . Published: 2026-04-01T08:05:34+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jianwen Liang; Li Lin; Junyan Lyu; Pujin Cheng; Xiaoying Tang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104345

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Self-supervised learning (SSL), which leverages large amounts of unlabeled data to reduce dependence on extensive annotations, has demonstrated remarkable transferability in medical image segmentation tasks. However, most existing SSL approaches for medical imaging are limited to single-modality data, overlooking the intrinsic complementarity among different imaging modalities. In brain tumor analysis, multimodal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) plays a crucial role in accurately delineating tumor subregions, as each modality provides distinct yet complementary information about tumor morphology and physiology. Therefore, effectively exploiting multimodal information during SSL has emerged as an important research focus for advancing brain tumor segmentation. In this study, we introduce a novel SSL framework, Modality Fusion and Masked Complementary Modeling (MF-MCM), developed for multimodal brain MRI. MF-MCM first performs modality-wise fusion through weighted sum of the input modalities to generate fused images equal in number to the input modalities. Subsequently, it applies complementary masking to the multichannel image constructed by concatenating the fused images, ensuring that the unmasked regions across all channels are non-overlapping and they jointly occupy the whole brain. Finally, by optimizing the multimodal image reconstruction and fusion coefficients regression tasks, the pre-training model is endowed with the ability to perceive fine-grained neuroanatomical details, integrate multimodal information, and achieve effective cross-modality interaction. The proposed SSL framework is fine-tuned and evaluated on four brain tumor segmentation datasets and one stroke lesion segmentation dataset. Results demonstrate that MF-MCM outperforms various state-of-the-art medical SSL methods while effectively reducing annotation requirements and maintaining robustness in scenarios where certain modalities are absent. Codes and pre-trained models will be available at https://github.com/liangjianwen01/MF-MCM .

item.title: Layout-Controlled Synthetic Data Generation for Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yu Qiao
Xiang Zhang
Chenji Wang
Shengmei Chen
Yanzi Shi
Hangzai Luo
Sheng Zhong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: High-performance remote sensing object detection typically requires vast datasets with annotations, but the collection and labeling of large-scale data is a costly and labor-intensive process. While generative models have shown promise in addressing the need for large-scale datasets, they heavily rely on high-quality labeled data and often overlook crucial aspects, such as the inter-object relationships and overall quality of the synthetic data. To this end, we propose Layout-controlled Synthetic Data Generation (LSDGen), a novel approach for generating high-fidelity remote sensing imagery for object detection. LSDGen consists of three key stages: (1) Relation-Constrained Layout Generation (RLGen), which constructs a category association matrix from benchmark data to generate both main and subcategories, as well as their spatial configurations, without relying on bounding-box annotations; (2) Image Synthesis via Layout-Conditional Diffusion Model, where the generated layout guides a diffusion model to produce high-quality remote sensing images; and (3) Instance-Centered Synthetic Data Filtering (ISDF), which evaluates the semantic consistency of individual instances in the synthesized images by calculating instance-level cosine similarity scores between visual and textual representations, filtering out low-confidence samples based on the average semantic score. Experimental results on the DIOR, DIOR-R, and HRSC datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves the detection performance of existing remote sensing image object detection models, outperforming state-of-the-art annotation-dependent synthesis approaches. Published: 2026-03-31T19:52:47+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.797 (must_read)
